data.title: 5 Clear Signs Of A Market Bubble
data.description:
The market continues to trade near all-time highs across all the major indexes. Equities continue to climb despite a clearly slowing jobs market. Job openings in this week's JOLTs report were lower than the number of unemployed individuals in the U.S. This is the first time this has occurred since April of 2021, over four years ago. Before the bell on Friday, the August BLS report showed only 22,000 positions created last month. Less than one third the consensus estimate of 75,000. This followed a disappointing July BLS reading as well as significant downward revisions to May and June jobs numbers.

Yet, the market continues remain near record levels. The rally appears to be primarily driven by the huge investments tech titans like Microsoft (MSFT), Alphabet (GOOG), Amazon (AMZN) and Meta Platforms (META) are making to build massive data centers and other AI related infrastructure and capabilities as well on increasing hopes that the Federal Reserve will finally start to reduce interest rates starting with the upcoming FOMC in mid-September.

So, how overvalued are U.S. equities? Significantly, in my opinion. In today's column, I look at five charts/metrics that should give any prudent person significant pause.

The U.S. makes up approximately 4.2% of the global population yet U.S. equities make up nearly half of all global stock market capitalization. Yes, New York City is the global financial capital and Silicon Valley is the epicenter of technology including the AI revolution. The greenback is the global reserve currency, at least for the moment. And the U.S. military is considered the preeminent military force on the planet.

However, do U.S. equities deserve a market capitalization over 5.5 times that of the European Union, which has over 100 million more citizens? Does it merit over five times the market capitalization of China with some 1.4 billion people?

I often point out the S&P 500 (SP500) trades at approximately 3.2 times revenues, which has only been exceeded briefly at the end of the Internet Boom.

However, this valuation looks downright attractive compared to the price to sales ratio of the NASDAQ (COMP:IND), which is now north 6.7 times.

Then we have the market cap to GDP ratio, also known as the "Buffett Indicator," given the weight the Oracle of Omaha gave to it during it his long, illustrious investing career. Is it any wonder why Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B) has built up record cash holdings in recent years before Mr. Buffett's retirement this year?

Let's move over to S&P 500 earnings yield (trailing 12-month earnings/S&P Index) which is just over 3.3%. The yield on the "risk free" 10-Year Treasury (US10Y) is currently 4.08%. This means, not only is there no "risk premium" to holding equities, it is actually negative.

Finally, we can look at the overall market via several arithmetic means. The chart above was calculated at the end of August. It consists of:

  • The Crestmont Research P/E ratio.
  • The cyclical P/E ratio using the trailing 10-year earnings as the divisor.
  • The Q ratio, which is the total price of the market divided by its replacement cost.
  • The relationship of the S&P composite price to a regression trendline.

These calculations show the market has never been valued higher.

An investor can believe one of two things with the markets trading at these levels. They can put their faith in the five of the most dangerous words in the English language "It is different this time," or acknowledge that the market is trading in extremely overbought territory.

I lean clearly to the latter conclusion. That leaves my portfolio roughly one quarter allocated in short term treasuries yielding four percent. And the rest is within covered call holdings around the few stocks in this market sporting reasonable valuations. That provides some downside risk mitigation. It also allows me to continue to participate in the rally should it continue before reality eventually arrives.

data.title: Novo Nordisk Vs Eli Lilly: Novo A Clear Winner
data.description:
Thesis Summary

Without a doubt, there are some great opportunities in the healthcare market right now.

But not all stocks are created equal, and in my opinion, Novo Nordisk (NVO) is arguably the best pharma stock you can own right now.

Many investors favour Eli Lilly (NYSE:LLY), but I will argue in this report that Novo outperforms Lilly in almost every aspect, while trading at a significantly lower price.

Let’s jump right in.

Product And Pipeline

Novo and Lilly are in competition in many different areas of the market, but the focus in recent years has been on weight loss drugs. Novo Nordisk has Ozempic and Wegovy, while Lilly has Mounjaro and Zepbound.

We can't deny that Eli Lilly showed some early superiority in this market, but all the recent data seems to suggest Novo may have the superior product now.

Wegovy has just been proven to be superior to Zepbound at reducing heart disease. Wegovy is also the first weight-loss medicine with an FDA label to reduce major adverse cardiovascular events (MACE).

On top of that, we know Novo also has approval to launch its own oral form of Wegovy.

In terms of future prospects, I won’t deny that Eli Lilly looks like it has a better pipeline.

Eli Lilly’s pipeline combines metabolic innovation with broad diversification. The key asset is orforglipron, an oral GLP-1 showing strong Phase 3 weight-loss data, with approvals expected from 2025 onward.

Lilly is also advancing donanemab for Alzheimer's, mirikizumab for IBD, pirtobrutinib in blood cancers, and lepodisiran, which dramatically lowers lipoprotein(a).

The company also has strategic deals like the Superluminal Medicines partnership and Verve’s CRISPR-based PCSK9 therapy to further expand its obesity and cardiovascular reach.

Novo Nordisk’s pipeline is more focused, centred on obesity and diabetes.

Flagship programs include CagriSema, showing ~23% weight loss in Phase 3, and amycretin, a next-gen GLP-1/amylin agonist with early strong results.

A high-dose oral Wegovy could become the first oral GLP-1 approved for obesity and CV risk reduction.

Novo also licensed a triple-agonist and struck a $2.2B deal with Septerna for GPCR-based therapies. Beyond GLP-1s, it’s adding insulin icodec, a once-weekly basal insulin, and concizumab, a haemophilia treatment—underscoring its strategy of deepening its metabolic moat while expanding selectively into adjacent areas.

All in all, Lilly is more diversified, but I don't think this is enough to justify the difference in valuation, especially when we look at growth and profitability metrics.

Growth And Profitability

Let’s start by looking at growth.

Yes, it is true that Eli Lilly has been growing more in the last year, with a whopping 36% revenue growth. However, if we expand into the last 3 and 5 years, it seems like growth has been quite similar.

The question here is whether Lilly can maintain this lead. The market seems to be betting hard that it will, but that is a risky bet in my opinion.

The downside here is that LLY is being more aggressive with its spending, and this is taking a toll on profitability.

NVO has a higher net income margin and generates almost $9 billion more in cash from operations.

This is perhaps one of the most compelling reasons to own Novo over Lilly.

Valuation

This then brings us to valuation, the other compelling reason to own Novo.

Novo trades at under half the FY1 P/E of LLY. EV/EBITDA is also much more attractive, and most of all, there’s a wild difference between the Price/Cash Flow.

Novo actually pays a healthy dividend, giving investors a nice 3% yield.

At these prices, the stock is undervalued, not just compared to Eli Lilly, but also when compared to the broader market.

I would attribute two main reasons to the discount versus Lilly.

  • Lilly is more aggressively pursuing growth

  • Novo is not an American company

But what investors see now as a drawback, they may soon begin to see as an advantage.

Novo’s Discount Could Become A Premium

The way I see it, Novo is the better buy, not just because of the discount, but indeed because of the reasons that it is discounted.

The fact that Novo is being more conservative with its spending and balance sheet will become a strength if we see a recession or market correction. Investors are pursuing growth aggressively now, but when the tides turn, the higher-flying names like Lilly will come down harder, while Novo will provide more stability.

On the other hand, investors have always placed a premium on American companies, but this is slowly changing. European stocks have seen strong gains and inflows in the last 12 months.

Global investors are seeking diversification from US assets and the US dollar, and with yields finally rising in Europe, capital is starting to flow its way.

This is a trend that may take years to develop, but ultimately, I think global portfolios are underweight European equities, and this is slowly changing.

Novo could become a big beneficiary, and in my opinion, this could naturally raise its valuation.

Risks

There are still risks to consider. Novo is going through an important transition, with a new CEO taking charge.

There’s also the issue of tariffs, which could definitely affect Novo. Even though Novo is not a US company, over half of its revenues come from the US. The current administration could certainly get in the way of Novo’s growth here.

Final Thoughts

In conclusion, I still think Novo is the clear winner between the two stocks and an overall great buy.

In the long run, this stock will recover and do very well, in my opinion.

data.title: The Fed's Beige Book Says It's Time To Invest For Stagflation
data.description:
There's been an increasing number of signs that suggest we are entering a period of stagflation. This is a very challenging dynamic for the Federal Reserve to grapple with, and the last time we had a serious stagflation issue was back in the 1970s. The stagflation in the 1970s was marked with challenging economic problems, including the 2 main pillars of stagflation, which are stagnant economic growth combined with persistently high inflation. There can also be other economic challenges during periods of stagflation, and this includes rising and/or high unemployment. The Federal Reserve's August Beige Book appeared to confirm stagflationary conditions. Let's take a closer look below at stagflation and review what assets do well in this environment and which ones typically do poorly.

What Drove Stagflation In The 1970s?

One big issue driving stagflation was the 1973 OPEC oil embargo, which caused a surge in oil prices, which led to higher production and transportation costs in the U.S.A. This surge in oil prices caused many companies to raise prices, which created inflation, and at the same time, many companies cut jobs, which led to rising unemployment. Many economists believe that the period of stagflation in the 1970s was also caused by expansionist monetary and fiscal policies that occurred in the late 1960s and early 1970s since the U.S. Government was trying to stimulate the economy and keep unemployment low. The problem is that this led to an excessive increase in the money supply, which fueled inflation. Paul Volcker was the Fed Chair at that time, and he raised the Federal Funds Rate to 20%, which resulted in a severe recession. However, the recession reset was just what was needed in order to take inflation down and bring the labor market back to a point where it could grow again.

What Is Driving Stagflation Today?

It seems that expansionist monetary and fiscal policies that occurred just before the stagflationary period in the 1970s were repeated with all the government stimulus that took place in response to the Covid pandemic. That seems to have contributed significantly to the rise in inflation, which was once labeled as being transitory. In response to this surge in inflation, the Federal Reserve raised interest rates, which finally seems to have reduced inflationary pressures. However, inflation is still near 3%, which is well above the Fed's target of 2%. In addition, there is concern that inflation could start to rise again due to the tariffs implemented by the Trump Administration. Another concern is that if the Fed goes forward with a rate cut in September, this could result in more inflation in the coming months. This could especially be an issue if the Fed cuts rates at a time when many asset prices are at or near record highs, which includes home prices and the stock market.

The Challenges For The Federal Reserve

Inflation has improved, but it could be reignited by rate cuts as well as tariffs. This desire to reduce inflation has to be balanced with another Fed mandate, which is to keep unemployment low. The current Consumer Price Index, or "CPI," is 2.7%, which is well above the Fed's target, and another CPI report will be released on September 11, 2025. With these inflation readings still being higher than the Fed's target level, it will be challenging to make any additional progress on inflation if the Fed decides to lower rates in September. It will also be challenging to make any further progress on inflation since many companies are starting to raise prices due to the cost increases that are being fueled by tariffs. Walmart (WMT) and other major retailers have said tariff costs are rising each week, and it plans to raise prices in response. I believe the impact of tariffs is just starting to trickle through the economy, so we have not yet felt the full impact, and this could reignite inflation in the coming months. Some people suggest that tariffs are "just" a one-time cost increase, but even if that is true, any cost increase still results in higher prices, and in addition to that, higher prices typically result in reduced demand, and that could lead to a stagnating economy.

The economy is slowing, and this can be seen by looking at the jobs market, manufacturing data, and more. This suggests we are potentially entering a period of stagnation, which, combined with persistent or rising inflation, is leading us to stagflation. The U.S. manufacturing sector has been contracting, and the ISM Manufacturing Index hit 48 in July, which represented the 5th consecutive monthly contraction and the worst decline since October 2024.

On August 1, the Bureau of Labor Statistics released the latest jobs report, which showed that July non-farm payroll growth was just 73,000. This was significantly below the expectations for 110,000. There was even worse news because the payrolls for May and June were revised down by a total of 258,000. The combination of this economic weakness along with persistent inflation that could rise as tariff expenses kick in points towards a period of stagflation.

The September 5th jobs report only confirms the recent shift in the labor market and seems to confirm a weakening trend. Expectations were at 75,000, but U.S. nonfarm payrolls rose by only 22,000 in August. Furthermore, unemployment rose to 4.3% from 4.2%. I believe this extremely weak jobs report opens the door to a negative payrolls number in the coming months, and this could spark a major growth scare for stock market investors and possibly lead to a recession.

These Assets Typically Outperform During Periods Of Stagflation

Hard assets and other raw materials such as gold, commodities, copper, and oil can act as a hedge against inflation and stagflation. Agricultural products also typically outperform in periods of stagflation. Defensive stocks such as consumer staples, utilities, healthcare, and value stocks tend to outperform as well. Investors tend to flock towards value stocks and companies that have pricing power and ones that provide products and services that are basic needs. By contrast, high-growth stocks and long-term bonds tend to underperform.

Gold has been surging, so I feel like it is a little late to buy now. However, platinum still appears undervalued, and I would buy this and other precious metals on pullbacks. I am finding buying opportunities in beaten-down value and healthcare stocks. This includes Centene (CNC), recently written about in this article, as well as UnitedHealth Group (UNH), in a separate article.

In Summary

I don't like the stock market in general now due to high valuations, so I would be a seller overall. However, I do think there are pockets of buying opportunities that continue to develop, and I am adding exposure to assets that can perform well as stagflationary pressures potentially increase. If stagflationary pressures continue, the Fed might have its hands tied, and ultimately, an economic reset (as was the case during the stagflation that started in the 1970s) that a recession can bring might be the only real way to clear the decks.

data.title: KLA: Decreased China Reliance Is Coming At A Cost
data.description:
Back in April of this year, I initiated KLA Corporation (NASDAQ:KLAC) at a buy rating. After a significant rally, I downgraded the stock to a neutral rating, citing weak Q4 guidance and a fair valuation. Since this article in June, the stock has declined by about 5% and so it seems that my shift in expectations was a timely one. In the below analysis, it is shown that Q4 results were strong. The company reported less reliance on China for revenues but looking forward, that could result in weak growth. In their Q1 guidance, it is shown that both top and bottom line growth is expected to decelerate substantially and so the near term outlook is rather undesirable. Nonetheless, the long term growth story for the company is likely still intact as AI chip demand remains strong. Therefore, I believe the P/E ratio's near 3-year median level correctly reflects the fundamentals. Thus, I have decided to reiterate my hold rating on the stock.

Revenue Analysis

KLA reported their FY2025 Q4 earnings at the end of July and top line results seem to be respectable at first glance. They generated a total of $3.175 billion in revenues, up 23.66% YoY. In the above growth chart, you can see that there was a notable deceleration from the previous quarter. This slowdown comes after five straight quarters of growth improvement and so it is perhaps an unfavorable turn of events. Still, KLA's top line beat estimates by a very decent margin of $95.11 million and so Q4 wasn't all that bad.

As you can see in the above chart, semiconductor process control is by far their largest segment by revenue. Therefore, I find it particularly encouraging that it was the segment that saw the strongest YoY growth. KLA is experiencing its best results in the area that matters most. Still, specialty semiconductor process and PCD and component inspection segments both saw respectable YoY growth and so there was widespread strength among their segments even if semiconductor process control was the strongest performer. That said, it you do compare these growth rates to the previous quarter, you do find some deceleration across the board. Investors should definitely keep that in mind.

In terms of their revenue by geographic region, there are some significant changes from the prior year period. For Q4, 30% of their revenues came from China. That is compared with 44% of revenues being from China in the year ago quarter. Even though there has been a thawing of relations between the U.S. and China in terms of semiconductors, I believe it is still favorable for chip related companies to have less reliance on China. While this may lower growth in the near term, you should also note the benefits of perhaps having less political exposure and therefore a more stable business. If you refer to the piechart for the prior year period you can see that revenues coming from Taiwan and Korea as a percentage of total revenues increased significantly. Overall, I see this pivot away from China reliance as being mixed for the company.

EPS Growth Headed South

In addition to a slowing of top line growth, KLA also saw a slowing of growth for their bottom line. They reported an adjusted EPS of $9.38, representing growth of 42.12% YoY. While that is still very respectable, it is also a considerable deceleration from Q3's 59.89%. Furthermore, this bottom line figure beat estimates by $0.83 to show outperformance as well. We'll discuss their FY2026 Q1 guidance later but as you can see in the chart above, EPS growth is expected to deteriorate significantly in the coming quarters according to consensus estimates. In fact, on the horizon, analysts expect negative growth for FY2026 Q4 and so the outlook doesn't seem to be too robust at this point.

Cash Flows Are Robust

While EPS growth is slowing, KLA's cash flows are remaining strong. In terms of operating cash flow, they reported $1.165 billion, up 30.51% YoY. With their CAPEX spending increasing by a considerable amount, FCF growth was 28% as shown above. FCFs are at the highest level of the past few years and the growth rate is actually an acceleration from the previous quarter. Overall, it seems that KLA's cash generation has been strong and even an increase in CAPEX has been unable to offset this strength. Furthermore, if you go to their FY2025 Q4 earnings presentation, you can see that the FCF margin of 31% for the LTM has been stable from CY24. In their Q4, they reported a FCF margin of 34% and so recently there has been cash flow robustness.

Decent Capital Returns

While FCFs are at multiyear highs, there are some mixed developments in terms of their capital returns to shareholders in Q4. Total dividend payout increased from $197.521 million to $253.965 million but share buybacks decreased from $470.226 million to $425.697 million. As a result, KLA only increased capital returns to shareholders by a net amount of $11.915 million. Considering the FCF growth rate that was stated above, this suggests that KLA is not overly eager to return capital. However, as you can see above, for share repurchases, LTM buybacks exceed the CY24 amount and dividends have a CAGR of 15% since 2006. This shows that in the grand scheme of things, capital returns are still decent and that the near term decrease in share buybacks may be temporary. Furthermore, their remaining share repurchase authorization is at $5.037 billion and so they might just be waiting for a more attractive price to buyback their stock.

Outlook Is Weak

KLA provided guidance for their FY2026 Q1. We'll discuss their revenue and adjusted EPS projections. At the midpoint, they are expecting revenues to total $3.15 billion. This would imply growth of just 10.84% YoY. That would be a rather large drop in growth from Q4's 23.66%. This could potentially be a concerning level of deceleration. Recall that I stated less reliance on China could be good for stability but bad for near term growth. This weak guidance likely reflects this. For adjusted EPS, they are expecting $8.53 at the midpoint. This would represent growth of 16.37%, a major deceleration from Q4's 42.12%. This projection is actually a bit lower than consensus expectations and so the outlook may have improved slightly since their earnings report. Nonetheless, I would say that this outlook is quite disappointing especially with demand for AI chips seemingly still holding rather strong.

Valuation Is Appropriate

KLA's P/E ratio has seen modest contraction since the start of July. Just very recently, the multiple dipped below the three year median. I believe this shows around fair valuation for the stock. Firstly, I believe overall the company is still well positioned to benefit from continued AI chip demand. That said, there are notable near term headwinds. As stated above, the company is becoming less reliant on China but at the same time, that may be a key cause of sluggish growth. The revenue and EPS deceleration as per their guidance is for sure unfavorable but when you take a longer term perspective, KLA is expected to be a key player in the semiconductor equipment and materials space. Therefore, I would say that the P/E ratio has correctly contracted to below the three year median but further contraction isn't likely as investors will point towards the intact long term growth story. Therefore, as a result of a balancing act between short term headwinds and long term potential, I believe the multiple's near median level shows a fair valuation for the stock.

Final Thoughts

I was pleasantly surprised at first to see that KLA's reliance on China has decreased. In their Q4, most financial metrics were still very respectable and so if they are able to keep up the growth while shifting their focus to other markets, that would be great. However, their guidance clearly suggests that's not the case. Top and bottom line growth is expected to drop sharply and I believe a pivot away from China could be a key cause of this weakness. However, with AI semiconductors still being in rather high demand around the rest of the world, it would be unwise to conclude that the growth story is over for KLA. Therefore, I believe the valuation's around median level reflects a balance of short term pain but the potential still for long term gains. As a result, I have decided to reiterate my neutral rating on KLA.

data.title: G-III: More Challenges Ahead This Year, And They Should Offer Better Opportunities
data.description:
G-III Apparel Group, Ltd. (NASDAQ:GIII) reported Q2 '26 results (ended July 2025).

The company is still growing in owned brands, and shedding license sales, for an aggregate negative print in the top line. The more challenging dynamic is seen in margins, particularly gross margins, hit hard by tariffs and mix.

The company reinstated EPS guidance, almost 40% lower than last year. The culprit again is the license losses and tariffs. Based on guidance, the name trades at 10/11x earnings.

I had not written about G-III since pre-tariffs, and although the price is similar to then, I believe the price is now only fair, reflecting the mix of internal positives and external negatives. I move the name to a Hold.

Q2 '26 results

License down: The company reported sales down 5% for the quarter and the same for the first half of the year. Although neither the release nor the call provides specific numbers on license versus owned, one can understand that it is licenses, mainly from PVH (PVH), that are shedding sales.

First, during the call, the company commented that owned brands are still growing, and are expected to close the year up MSD. Second, we know that the company is slowly releasing the PVH licenses, and during the call, it commented on how this is already affecting retailers' willingness to purchase from G-III.

On last year's 10-K, licenses had only represented a $100 million headwind, whereas it is very possible that this and next year are much worse. In FY25 (calendar 2024), the company generated 34% of sales from PVH (down 7pp YoY already), or about $1 billion in sales. The licenses are expected to bottom at $400 million in FY27 (calendar 2026). That is, the company is still waiting for a $600 million revenue headwind in FY26 and FY27.

For Q1 '25 (the 10-Q for 2Q25 has not been published yet) licensed brands shed $60 million in sales, and owned brands grew $30 million. It is possible that the situation is similar in Q2 '25.

The company is adding more licenses (BCBG and Converse last year), but these are much smaller than PVH.

Brands up, more moderate: The company's owned brands continue to expand between HSD and 30%+, depending on the brand (DK, which was re-launched last year, is growing faster, DKNY or KL, which are more established, much slower).

I have commented positively on the company's management of these brands for several quarters, and continue to believe that. The brands are growing, and the company is active in searching for new licenses (like more Villebrequin clubs, or jewelry for DK). Being mostly wholesale brands, the company continues to add POS and expand categories (DK expected to grow 40% in FY26, KL has added 150 POS for Fall/Winter, DKNY is expanding in Europe).

Still, management's comments about the quarter left a negative taste, because the company is guiding for MSD owned-brand growth instead of last year's HSD. The culprit, according to management, is the macro environment. I believe this is true, with a significant impact on pricing and disposable income, plus retailer risk-aversion. Still, it makes me more wary about G-III's near-term future.

Margins very challenged: The worst part of the release was seen in gross margins, down 200bps. This is not super terrible, given the environment, and the fact that GIII is a wholesaler and works with higher margins (40% last year) than a retailer.

However, it is the factor that has the highest impact on earnings. Further, the impact is yet to be fully felt, as tariffs are only starting to impact CoGS. The company guided for margins down 300bps for the year, with most of the impact felt in Q4.

SG&A was down in dollars but deleveraged, unable to offset the impact from lower sales and margins.

A hard year ahead

The company is one of the public wholesalers more directly exposed to China, because of its participation in more complex products like leatherwear, outerwear, bags, and shoes. Of course, this is a market-wide reality, and G-III is not the only company impacted, particularly in the more premium segments of the market, but the impact of tariffs will be felt, at about $150 million according to management (more than 10% of last year's gross profitability). The company expects to offset this via pricing, but the impact will still be relevant.

This has added to the loss of the PVH licenses, which will really decrease by FY27 (many of the licenses are only lost in December 2025, although the company is already feeling the impact in retail).

The result is a big impact on earnings, which will be felt particularly strongly in 2H26 and early FY27 (early calendar 2026).

Valuation will present better opportunities later

I believe G-III has a lot to like. It has premium owned brands and a lot of experience in the higher-end wholesale apparel industry. It sports very good gross margins, has no debt, has insider ownership (insiders recently purchased some shares during the June post-Q1 '26 fall), has no debt and has almost 10% of its market cap in net cash.

However, the name will probably face its hardest period between fiscal Q3 '26 and Q2 '27, combining the impact of tariffs, disposable income headwinds, and the loss of the PVH licenses. It will be a time of flux, and potentially not very positive news. Only looking at Q3 '26, the company has already guided for sales down HSD to DD.

In this context, the name trades at a guided P/E of about 10/11x. The stock is hovering around $27.5, and guided EPS for the year is $2.55/2.75 (guidance was reinstated, previously removed after tariffs were announced in Q1 '26).

This is not particularly high, but it is clearly not discounted either. The company's FY27 earnings are hard to estimate at this point in time. Assuming margins recover to 10/12%, and revenues fall to $2.5 billion, we could be talking of net earnings of $200 million, which does not compare positively with a market cap above $3 billion.

Further, this is all dependent on how the economy develops forward. It might be harder to grow (or even maintain) sales, and harder to regain margins.

More importantly, I believe the name is likely to offer better opportunities down the road, as potentially there's more fear and panic when Q3 '26 or Q4 '26 results are announced showing challenges in margins, or when early FY27 results show more challenged revenues.

For that reason, I believe it is better to wait on GIII, and move the name from Buy to Hold.

data.title: Housing: 2006 Redux
data.description:
I have been writing about the deteriorating residential real estate market frequently recently. I am doing so for several reasons. First, U.S. residential real estate is one of the largest asset classes on the globe. Second, home prices impact consumer confidence significantly. With most American consumers already struggling and accounting for nearly 70% of U.S. economic activity, this is always worth keeping a close eye on.

Unfortunately, the U.S. is very likely headed toward a significant housing adjustment. Home prices soared after Covid, powered largely by the Federal Reserve's easy money and QE policies that brought average mortgage rates down below 33% for the first time in American history. The central bank and U.S. government also upped the money supply by close to 40% following Covid. This significantly boosted the prices for almost every asset class, including equities and cryptocurrency.

I believe the housing sector is on the verge of having to pay the piper for that recklessness. Will the coming housing crash be as severe as the Housing Bust that set off the Great Financial Crisis? I am not forecasting that kind of scenario. But let's not fool ourselves, it is likely going to be a significant event given the dismal current levels of housing affordability.

When I write these articles, too many readers tend to get hung up on pointing out how conditions in the market are different than they were in 2005-2008. And I couldn't agree more on some levels. Many homeowners either have no mortgages on their houses or are sitting on large amounts of equity in their properties. The latter's biggest problem is they have the "golden handcuffs" from ultra-low mortgage rates. The "liar loans" and "no income verification" mortgages that helped trigger the Housing Bust are a thing of the past. This housing crash will not be driven by "subprime" this time around.

However, there are many similarities between where we are now in the housing market and the conditions that were becoming apparent in 2006, just before the big swoosh down in housing values. As you can see above, the months of new home inventory supply is moving sharply higher, just as they did in 2006.

Average home prices have started to fall in many regions of the U.S. Some markets, like Oakland and Austin, have seen average single-family home prices fall by more than 20% from their peak, others like New Orleans and San Francisco are not far behind.

Let's take a look at the current state of the market from a recent synopsis of the first quarter mortgage market. First, note that the mortgage market overall appears in good shape. Only 2.1% of mortgage holders are currently "underwater" as of the end of the first quarter. This compares to a record 26% of homeowners that had no equity in their homes in 2009. This will obviously go higher if average home prices deteriorate in the coming quarters.

Serious delinquencies rose to 0.86% of all mortgages from just 0.70% in the fourth quarter of 2024. However, foreclosures rose nearly 50% in the first quarter sequentially from 4Q2024. This will substantially worsen in the quarters ahead. As I noted in a recent article, some 11% of the approximately 7.8 million FHA mortgages were delinquent as of the end of 2024.

It is important to note that these are low-money-down mortgages. In addition, delinquency rates have been kept bottled up by Covid-related loss mitigation efforts that have kept mortgage holders in their homes even when they haven't made their payments. These programs largely come to an end on Sept. 30. This should lead to a spike in foreclosures by the first or second quarter of next year.

The delinquency rate on VA mortgage loans, also low-money-down mortgages, was 4.7% at the end of 2024. While much lower than the FHA delinquency rate, these mortgages are vulnerable to quickly going underwater as home prices fall. VA loans were responsible for just over $18 billion in mortgage volume, numbering just over 46,000 mortgages in July.

It is important to note that the institutions holding mortgages have changed significantly since the Housing Boom & Bust. Banks are much less exposed to the mortgage market than they were before the Housing Bust. Out of the 10 largest mortgage originators in 2024, only four were traditional banks with a full suite of financial services. Bank of America (BAC) was the only one in the top six, at No. 4.

It is probably important to note two things. Non-bank lenders do not have access to low-cost deposits like banks do, and they receive less stringent regulatory oversight. In addition, approximately 70% of mortgage loans are then packaged to Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac now. Both of these institutions are now government-owned instead of private, before the Housing Bust, which required the U.S. government to take over these institutions in 2008.

There has been much discussion of bringing these entities public again recently. Investors should note that net income in the second quarter at Fannie Mae fell from $4.48 billion in the same period a year ago to $3.32 billion. The main factor that drove the decline in earnings was a $946 million increase to credit loss provisions, hardly a good harbinger for the overall housing market.

Given the increased roles of government agencies and institutions like FHA, VA, Fannie Mae, and Freddie Mac, Melody Wright, one of the few analysts who correctly called the Housing Bust before it happened, has dubbed the danger to the housing market from this dependence "Government Subprime." She has become more and more dire on the state of the housing market over the past year.

What impacts will falling home prices have on the overall economy? How will they compare to 2006-2009? What are the ramifications for the markets? I don't know, but investors are likely to see those questions answered in 2026 and 2027.

data.title: AOD: Inconsistent Dividend Coverage And Premium Valuation
data.description:
Overview

As markets continue to trade near all-time highs, I believe that income funds are an efficient way to hedge your portfolio against the continued uncertainty and volatility of the market. Additionally, abrdn Total Dynamic Dividend Fund (NYSE:AOD) provides investors with some global exposure that can reduce reliance on US based equities. When I previously covered AOD, I issued a hold rating due to the less appealing valuation and inconsistent NAV growth. Since the time of my last coverage, AOD has released an updated semi-annual report that prompted me to revisit this fund to provide some updated insights into its performance and outlook for the remainder of 2025.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that AOD's share price has increased by about 1.4%. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, AOD's total return jumps up above 16% over the same time frame. One of the fund's main appeals is the generous starting dividend yield of 13.2% and monthly payouts. However, the distribution coverage has been a bit inconsistent in recent years, which makes me cautious about the sustainability of the current payouts.

Due to the recent market rally, AOD's price now trades at the lowest discount to NAV pricing over the last decade. Therefore, I remain cautious about accumulating shares at the current price range. Although AOD provides instant exposure to some of the highest quality growth companies in the world, the fund's focus on income generation has translated to the fund underperforming more traditional ETF counterparts. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying fund strategy and performance risk of the fund.

Fund Strategy & Performance Risk

According to the most recent fact sheet, AOD now has total managed assets of $1B that are spread across a diverse range of equities. AOD also has a management fee of 1.03%, excluding all other expenses. The fund aims to provide exposure to companies that can help the fund achieve its goal of a high dividend income and capital appreciation over time. Additionally, AOD provides global exposure to assets that are based outside of the US, which makes it a great choice for investors looking to expand the diversity of their portfolio. The breakdown by region is as follows:

  • North America: 61.9%
  • Europe (excluding UK): 17.5%
  • Asia Pacific (excluding Japan): 11.2%
  • United Kingdom: 4.8%
  • Japan: 2.0%
  • Latin America: 1.7%

Looking at the breakdown by sector, we can see that AOD is most heavily allocated towards Information Technology companies that make up 23.2% of assets. This is followed by exposure to Financials and Industrials investments, which accounts for 17.7% and 11% of assets respectively. We can see that the fund provides exposure to some of the highest quality growth companies in the world. For instance, Microsoft (MSFT) is the largest individual position at 4.3% of assets. This also includes exposure to other large-cap companies such as Broadcom (AVGO), Alphabet (GOOG), Apple (AAPL), and Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) just to name a few.

Since AOD doesn't utilize any sort of leverage or fancy option strategies to generate its income, AOD needs to actively sell off portions of the upside gains that its holdings experience. Therefore, AOD may not be capable of participating in market rallies and may not be the best choice for investors that are seeking the highest possible total return. Just to demonstrate this, I pulled in a performance graph of AOD over the last decade.

As we can see below, AOD's share price has only increased by 15.4% over the last ten years. Therefore, investors wouldn't have been able to participate in the bull market over the last decade. However, the fund's total return jumps up above 179% over the same time frame when including all distributions that were paid out. So investors should anticipate that the majority of their returns are reliant on the fund's ability to continue paying out a high rate of distributions.

The problem is that a higher dividend yield doesn't automatically translate to a higher total return over time. If you are a younger investor with a longer time horizon ahead of you, you may be better off with a more traditional ETF that is capable of providing capital appreciation. For instance, something as straightforward as a total stock market ETF would get the job done, such as:

  • Vanguard Total Stock Market Index Fund ETF (VTI)
  • Vanguard Total World Stock Index Fund ETF (VT)

As we can see below, AOD is outperformed by more traditional ETFs like VTI or VT. VTI is a total stock market ETF that essentially provides exposure to the entire US stock market. VT is the total world stock index, which provides global exposure and may be a more accurate parallel to AOD. By focusing on income rather than growth, you may be actively choosing to have less money at the end of the road.

Valuation

At the time of my last coverage, AOD traded at a discount to NAV of about 8.45%. Following the market rally over recent months, AOD now trades at a smaller discount to NAV of approximately 5.81%. However, AOD now trades at the smallest discount level experienced over the last ten year period. In an effort to better conceptualize the current discount in relation to its ten year range, I added the red line to the chart below. Therefore, it becomes difficult to recommend accumulating shares at the current price level.

Since the time of my last coverage, AOD has released an updated semi-annual report that is dated through the end of April 2025. Net investment income amounted to $0.26 per share, while AOD experienced net realized losses of $0.19 per share. However, I believe that the results of this semi-annual report are skewed due to the timing. Markets were declining throughout March and April of 2025, in response to President Trump's tariffs. However, markets rallied in the following months and rapidly recovered, which isn't captured here in the reporting.

Over prior years, AOD has demonstrated that it's capable of generating a consistent range of net investment income. However, net investment income isn't strong enough to support distributions on its own. This leaves the fund partially reliant on net realized gains to fuel NAV growth and support distribution health. The problem is that the fund's net realized gains are dependent on positive market conditions so that AOD can sell securities at a higher valuation than they were purchased. This dependence has slowed the rate of AOD's underlying NAV growth. For reference, here are the year-end annual NAV amounts:

  • 2020: $10.16 per share
  • 2021: $12.95 per share
  • 2022: $10.05 per share
  • 2023: $9.90 per share
  • 2024: $11.15 per share
  • 2025 (semi-annual): $10.55 per share

Dividend Sustainability

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.10 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 13.2%. If we multiply this monthly distribution by twelve months, we get an estimated annual payout of $1.20 per share. Referring back to the same semi-annual report, AOD has demonstrated that it can generate a sufficient level of earnings when market conditions are positive. For reference, here are the annual earnings amounts over the last few years:

  • 2020: ($0.20) per share
  • 2021: $3.57 per share
  • 2022: ($2.12) per share
  • 2023: $0.63 per share
  • 2024: $2.18 per share
  • 2025 (semi-annual): $0.07 per share

As we can see, AOD has only been able to generate earnings that outpace the distribution amount in two of the last five full-year periods. While there are periods where AOD was able to generate earnings high enough to support several years worth of distributions, such as in 2021, this inconsistency makes it hard to get a sense of how secure the distributions are. Looking back over the last ten years, AOD has managed to keep payouts consistent and even raised them towards the end of 2024.

According to the most recent section19(a) notice, however, the distributions are fueled by a combination of net investment income and return of capital. Since 59% of the distributions come from net investment income, most of the income received would be classified as ordinary dividends, which have the highest tax burden for investors. Therefore, based on your specific tax bracket and the amount of capital invested, a position in AOD may be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account to offset potential tax consequences for investors.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my hold rating on AOD due to the smallest discount to NAV pricing over the last decade. Although the fund provides a diverse exposure to high quality growth companies across the world, the fact that AOD is reliant on positive net realized gains limits the ability to participate in capital appreciation over time. Since the fund has to sell off portions of this upside to pay out distributions, this causes AOD to underperform more traditional ETFs that do a better job at capturing market growth. Lastly, the fund has proven that it's capable of out-earning the distributions, but the coverage has remained inconsistent over the last few years.

data.title: Tesla Hits My Target: Now The Stock Is Running On Robotics Hype (Downgrade)
data.description:
Tesla, Inc. (NASDAQ:TSLA) has gained 18.9% since my last report, outperforming the S&P 500’s (SP500) 17.7% gain and supporting my Buy rating. The company is expanding beyond electric vehicles by accelerating investment and development in robotics, artificial intelligence, and energy storage, aiming to reduce reliance on car sales. While these opportunities could transform Tesla into a diversified technology leader, investors should also consider how current revenue streams are performing and what Q2 2025 earnings reveal about execution. In this report, I review Tesla’s latest results, the strategic development, and update my price target.

Robotaxi, Optimus, And FSD

One of the developments that investors are optimistic about is Robotaxi. Tesla finally launched services in June in Austin. What should be kept in mind is that it was launched using a small number of Model Y vehicles and not with the steering wheel-less Cybercab. I would say that for something that has been rumored to be close since 2019, it was a bit of a soft launch. From here, the company can obviously expand the service area and fleet and roll out to other areas, which is also in the planning, but Tesla is not the first mover in this space by any means.

The launch in Austin did cause a delay in Autopilot development, and the company is working with regulators on the rollout of full self-driving, or FSD. Tesla is taking an incremental approach with FSD, which makes sense, but until full self-driving is actually cleared by regulators, FSD is more of a Level 2 system than a Level 4-5 system, which is truly autonomous. So, Tesla is not there yet, and the big question is whether or when they will be. The path to FSD has been highlighted for almost 10 years now, and the company is still not there.

The big game changer for Tesla would be Optimus, which is Tesla’s humanoid robot. Tesla expects prototypes this year and scaled production next year. CEO Elon Musk expects to be able to produce 1 million units a year within five years. That is an optimistic target. The way I view it, all of Tesla’s “new projects” can be transformative, especially when coupled to the same neural network and AI, but many of their initiatives have been talked about for years and have a rather slow path to reality.

Tesla Earnings: Back To Reality

While Tesla has big plans, we see that the reality of the existing business is less rosy. Total sales declined 12% but still exceeded analysts' expectations. Automotive revenues declined 16%, driven by lower sales volumes and a lower average selling price due to the delivery mix, but were up 19% sequentially, driven by 14% higher deliveries and a higher average selling price driven by Model Y. Due to repeal of the EV credit, Tesla has dialed back production in the US, and the change of penalties for emission standards to zero will also drive lower revenues on the sale of regulatory credits going forward. Putting it together, ASP and gross margins may improve due to higher share of Model Y vehicles in the mix, but tariffs and lower volumes provide a drag.

Energy generation and storage revenues declined 7% due to lower average selling prices for the Megapack, partially offset by higher Powerwall deployment. This was partially offset by higher services revenues. Gross margins decreased 71 basis points due to lower sales, average sales prices, and tariff impacts. There was a $300 million additional tariff impact during the quarter, with a $200 million impact on automotive and a $100 million impact on the energy business. Despite tariff headwinds, gross margins improved from 24.6% to 30.3%, driven by lower unit costs.

If we put it all together, the automotive business is under clear pressure, while the energy generation and storage business is scaling well to the extent the benefits of lower costs are offsetting tariff impacts and lower ASP for Megapack.

Tesla Stock Is Getting Ahead Of Itself

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

Tesla stock has reached my $328.87 price target. As I noted before, there is significant pressure on the company’s performance and analyst expectations going forward. Compared to my prior assessment, we see an additional 5.6% downward revision to 2025 EBITDA, and in the years after, the estimates contracted by 12.1% and 7.6%. As a result, total EBITDA generation is expected to be 8.6% lower, and CAGR has been adjusted from 15.9% to 12.6%. Free cash flow generation is also under pressure with a 16.4% downward adjustment this year, 30.7% next year, and 21.6% in the year after. This constitutes a 23.6% total decline, and CAGR has been adjusted from 40% to 30%.

Even at the expanded trading multiple of 57.1x, we no longer see upside for FY26. Therefore, I am adjusting my rating to Hold.

Conclusion: Tesla Stock Was Expensive, Remains Expensive

Tesla is one of the stocks you will probably never find cheap. That didn’t prevent me from issuing a Buy rating in my prior assessment. However, the price target has been reached and exceeded, and the expectations for this year and the years ahead have been revised downward significantly. As a result, I have adjusted the rating to Hold, noting that successes such as Robotaxi, Optimus, and FDS are either underwhelming or non-existent at this stage. In my view, a lot of optimism is also built on what I believe are rather vague comments from Elon Musk on Optimus development and scaling, as well as the robotaxi project. Furthermore, successful unsupervised FSD does not seem to be as close as some want it to be.

data.title: Are Rate Cuts The Right Move?
data.description:
Relative to economic growth, rate cuts are warranted but not essential

Headline economic data is not screaming out for rate cuts, but in a world where the Federal Reserve has a tendency to favour dovishness over hawkishness and ever-increasing fiscal dominance, easier monetary policy can easily be justified.

If we start by looking at the historical relationship between changes in the policy rate and coincident economic growth, it looks as though the policy rate is probably about where it should be, all else equal.

The same can largely be said when looking at the policy rate relative to nominal GDP growth.

What I view as the main takeaway from these two charts is that compared to coincident economic data, monetary policy is far from loose, and should it weaken modestly from here, there is no reason why the Fed shouldn’t cut rates. Inflation targets be damned.

It is well understood now the US economy has proven remarkably robust over the past three years. Higher inflation, higher interest rates and higher tariff rates have not been able to derail what has so far been a “Roaring 2020s”. Even now, with growth starting to slow on the margins, the Conference Board’s Coincident Economic Index is still hovering around its 20-year average.

Headlining the robust US economy has been a combination of higher average consumption, strong income growth and a tight jobs market.

However, consumption and income growth have declined notably over the past few years to slightly below-average levels (though still high relative to recent decades). Employment growth has also now decelerated to slightly below-average levels. Industrial production growth has also been relatively weak over the past few years. Growth is largely decelerating, but remains far from recessionary levels.

Such weakness is enough to justify rate cuts in isolation. This is especially true when we look at some of the key cyclical drivers of the business cycle. Business investment growth has declined to near-negative levels, while growth in construction spending (and notably, residential construction spending) is negative. Importantly, cyclical employment growth (that is, construction, trucking and manufacturing employment) has declined notably over the past few months.

These cyclical areas of the economy tend to be more closely linked to monetary policy (particularly as it relates to business investment and household construction). As a result, weakness therein is more likely to result in a dovish monetary policy tilt.

On a forward-looking basis, lead indicators are pointing to a modest decline in growth moving forward.

Currently, the biggest areas of weakness on a leading basis appear to be a mixed outlook for the manufacturing cycle. The outlook for the housing market also appears somewhat challenged. Capex spending is also likely to be subdued going forward as businesses cut back on investment thanks somewhat to lingering tariff uncertainty, while the positive impulse from the wealth effect is also seemingly behind us.

However, the outlook for the economy is still not recessionary. High-frequency economic data suggests Q3 GDP growth is likely to come in around the 5% mark.

Moreover for the Fed, the labour market is likely to continue to hold together reasonably well, though weaken at the margins. However, the Fed doesn’t need a recession to cut rates.

The jobs market is holding up, but is weaker on the margins

Moderate weakness is the key here. On an outright basis, the jobs market is doing fine. If we compare the rate of change in the policy rate relative to employment, monetary policy is about where it should be.

As a result, should the Fed be wary that employment growth is likely to trend modestly lower from here, then cuts are perfectly warranted to keep this ratio in-line. Even without declining employment growth and a couple of rate cuts, monetary policy will be far from being too loose.

Importantly for the Fed, headline employment data has exhibited a notable deceleration in momentum over the past few quarters. As we can see below, this is true for both employment and wage growth.

On a leading basis, I am still not convinced we see enough of a slowdown in the labour market to warrant more than maximum two to three rate cuts, let alone a recession.

Employment growth lead indicators may have stopped accelerating of late, but they are still not rolling over to any notable degree. As such, based on what the leading data is currently telling me, I don’t foresee a sufficient rise in the unemployment rate to warrant significant easing by the Fed.

Having said that, the outlook for wage growth appears more favourable from a monetary policy perspective, as we can see below.

Much will depend on how August’s payroll figures come in. With the Fed likely to cut in their September meeting, it will take a hell of an upside surprise for this to change. Just don’t expect the labour market to provide a pathway to more than one or two rate cuts in 2025.

Inflation should be a problem for the Fed

Where the economic data is more hawkish is on the inflation front. More specifically, relative to the Fed's stated 2% target and relative to the outlook for inflation, inflation data is simply not supportive of rate cuts.

We can see below all primary measures of headline, core and sticky inflation remain above the Fed’s 2% target.

What’s more, inflation is only going to accelerate from here. My composite lead indicator is pointing to ~4% headline CPI at some point over the coming couple of quarters.

However, I am not expecting this forthcoming rise in inflation to be particularly long-lived, at least relative to 2021-2023 data. Unlike that period, we are not seeing a universal rise in individual inflation lead indicators. And, of those which have been rising, we are seeing signs some may have peaked (notably prices paid, food and energy). There are also few signs of a forthcoming rise in services inflation, which will limit the upside to any inflation spike, at least for now.

How long this acceleration lasts is perhaps the key to how inflation plays a role in the Fed’s decision-making process. If the Fed believes this is largely a tariff-driven price response that is not likely to be permanent or has characteristics of demand-push inflation, then I suspect they will use such claims as means to ease.

It is also worth mentioning that relative to Core CPI, the policy rate is slightly on the tighter side of the ledger. History suggests that it is perfectly reasonable for minor cuts to the policy rate given current levels of Core CPI and the Fed Funds Rate.

Room to ease

As we have seen, so long as the Fed is happy to temporarily ignore inflation data, then there is room to ease.

That’s the key - ignoring inflation data (at least temporarily) is the key to rate cuts. If we look at my Fed Policy Regime model, which incorporates economic, employment, inflation and financial market data, then current data from all the various inputs is suggesting monetary policy should remain neutral for the time being.

However, if we remove the inflation and financial market components from this model, such that it only incorporates headline economic and employment data, then it is right on the cusp of the easing zone.

The market is seemingly betting on the Fed temporarily ignoring inflation data. Futures markets are pricing in around two cuts over the next six months and four to five over the next 12.

I’d still take the under on five cuts over the next 12 months, given the non-recessionary outlook for economic growth and employment and the forthcoming rise in inflation. But two cuts over the next six months appears a near certainty.

Summary, outlook and key macro notes:

  • Due to historically strong household and corporate balance sheets, persistent fiscal support, lax immigration and labour hoarding, the US economy has continued to be resilient over the past two years despite a multi-decade high in interest rates. Trump 2.0’s tariff and DOGE policies do not yet appear to be bringing down the US economy, and look increasingly unlikely to do so as Trump pivots away from trade wars to more populist-type policies.

  • Households and corporations have been able to weather the storm of rising interest rates, leading to an income-driven business cycle. It is difficult for the US to enter recession given the excellent state of household balance sheets, while strong corporate balance sheets are also supportive of the labour market. However, the best of the resilient US economy is now behind us.

  • Overall, the US economy continues to grow at an average pace, with hard data far from recessionary levels. The labour market is showing signs of weakness (particularly in cyclical sectors), but remains far from recessionary levels.

  • Economic lead indicators continue to hold up and are not signaling any notable rise in unemployment or economic weakness forthcoming. However, US economic and employment growth is likely to slow on the margins, though will probably continue to surprise to the upside.

  • The big fall in the dollar during the first half of 2025 is also very supportive of global growth and global liquidity with a lag, so the second half of 2025 should continue to be supported by a weaker dollar.

  • Trump wants a US economy that is not reliant on government spending, and is instead driven by the private sector. However, thanks to the bond market turmoil, mid-term election pressures and the checks and balances of the US political system, Trump appears to be pivoting away from his pro-tariff and deficit reduction policies and back toward more populist policies that got him elected in the first place. This likely means the negative impacts from tariffs and DOGE are likely to be much smaller than expected, while the “Big Ugly Bill” is likely to continue the fiscal impulse into 2026 and 2027 - a supportive backdrop for the US economy. He now wants to grow his way out of the debt problem, which is a positive backdrop for risk-taking over the medium term.

  • The Federal Reserve appears intent on cutting rates. Two to three cuts can easily be justified based on employment and economic data, but not on inflation data. Monetary easing and a non-recessionary outlook for the economy is also a favourable backdrop for risk assets of the medium term.

  • It is the bond market, not the stock market, that represents the Fed and Fiscal put. Both Trump and Powell have made this very clear.

  • Overall, the US economy and labour market should continue to hold together better than expected, though should see some weakness at the margins.

Biggest risks facing markets in 2025/26:

  • The Fed being unable to sufficiently ease monetary policy, thanks to above-target and accelerating inflation. However, it is important to remember that historically, fiscal dominance has generally led to a Federal Reserve that is supportive of the Treasury by having a dovish reaction function despite data to the contrary. Trump appointing a known dovish Fed chair may well be a right tail risk not fully anticipated by markets.

  • The repatriation of global capital out of US equity and bond markets back to domestic markets. The rise in European fiscal spending and the monetary policy nominalisation in Japan are both legitimate dynamics that represent falling demand for US Treasuries and risk assets.

  • The forthcoming rise in inflation could upset markets later in 2025. However, this dynamic is now fully anticipated by markets, so it may not be the risk it once was.

data.title: August Jobs Report: Another Weak Report Heightens Pressure For Rate Cuts
data.description:
The August jobs report ends a busy week of data showing just how fragile the labor market is. Hiring momentum has slowed, gains continue to be concentrated in just a handful of industries, and public sector employment is beginning to meaningfully weigh on the headline numbers. The household data points to a gradual loosening in the labor supply, with specific age groups facing more pain than others. Together, these dynamics highlight a labor market that is no longer uniformly strong but softening in key areas that will spur the Federal Reserve into action.

Establishment Data

US nonfarm payroll employment came in weak again in August, increasing by just 22,000, which is a deceleration from the 79,000 jobs added in July and well below the consensus estimate of 75,000. This number looks more like the soft prints in May and June than the bounce back in July, suggesting that there is a real slowdown in hiring that can’t be ignored. With this print, the three-month average of job gains remains below 30,000 for the second straight month. The details of the report tell a similar story of the headline employment number being carried by a few industries.

The first thing to note in the details is that there was a divergence between private and public sector hiring in August. Private payrolls increased 38,000, still below expectations of around 75,000 but notably stronger than the headline number. This means that government payrolls declined by -16,000, the second largest month behind May 2025 in the last three years. The shedding of jobs in the public sector was driven by the federal government as the Trump administration’s efforts to downsize the federal workforce continue to impact the labor market. So far this year, government payrolls (federal, state, and local) have only increased by 8,000. In the same period last year, government payrolls increased by 323,000.

Within the private sector, hiring conditions weren’t particularly strong. The goods sector saw a decline in employment for the fourth straight month at -25,000, which is also the worst decline since October 2024 and the second worst since March 2023. All three industries tracked in the goods sector lost jobs, including the manufacturing sector (-12,000), which is also on a four-month streak of job losses. Offsetting the decline in goods sector employment was a 63,000 increase in the services sector, which looks like a solid increase, but the sector’s gains were driven primarily by two industries: education & health services (+46,000) and leisure & hospitality (+28,000). The other services industries saw job losses or no growth, including professional services (-17,000) and information (-5,000). The conclusion is one that we have seen before: payroll employment gains are being propped up by two industries, education & health services and leisure & hospitality.

Household Data

Alongside a weakening in hiring came an increase in the unemployment rate in the Household data. The unemployment rate ticked up 0.1 ppts to 4.3% in August, in line with the consensus forecast. The more precise increase in the rate was about 7.5 bps, or an increase from 4.25% to 4.32%. In terms of raw numbers, there was a 148,000 increase in the unemployed population and an even larger 288,000 increase in the employed population. And overall, there was a strong 436,000 increase in the labor force, which caused the labor force participation rate to tick up 0.1 ppt to 62.3%, reversing a trend of a participation rate that has been falling since April. These general statistics all suggest that the supply side of the labor market is loosening but not at an alarming rate.

While the overall unemployment rate only saw a gradual increase, there were some interesting dynamics for certain age groups.

  • The first thing to note was a -1.3 ppts drop in the unemployment rate for the 16 to 19-year-old segment after spiking 1.8 ppts over the summer. While this could be tracked to changes related to the school year, this data is seasonally adjusted, so volatility like this shouldn’t happen. I hypothesize that summer employment opportunities must have been thinner than usual for that group, which resulted in the exaggerated moves.

  • Moving up to the next age segment, we see an even more concerning trend. The unemployment rate for 20- to 24-year-olds, correlating to individuals getting out of high school or college to join the workforce, jumped 1.3 ppts to 9.2%, the highest since May 2021. A surge in joblessness in this segment typically only happens when there is a recession coming or currently happening. However, there is a chance that this instance could be an exception since AI is increasingly encroaching on young professionals’ abilities to find an entry-level job.
  • Finally, there are the prime age (25 to 54 years) and 55+ age segments, which both stand out as stronger than the two younger segments. In August, the prime-age unemployment rate remained unchanged at 3.6%, even though there was an increase of about 50,000 in the raw values. The monthly trend was similar for the 55+ segment, where the unemployment rate remained at 2.9%. Most significantly, both of these segments have seen no increase in their unemployment rates in the last year, suggesting that the labor market for more skilled and established workers is actually quite solid.

These details matter because they show how broad labor market trends are playing out across different groups. In effect, there are two labor markets unfolding: one where younger workers are struggling to find jobs and falling behind in starting their careers and financial lives, and another where prime-age and older workers feel relatively secure and are not facing the same risks of layoffs. As noted earlier, weakness in younger cohorts has historically been a reliable signal of recession risk, often more telling than a small uptick, such as a 0.1 ppt increase, in the overall unemployment rate.

Wage Growth

The wage growth data from the Establishment data was mostly consistent with the cooling in hiring seen in the headline job gains numbers. Average hourly earnings (AHE) increased 0.3% MoM in August, and the annual increase slowed from 3.9% YoY in July to 3.7% YoY, matching June for the lowest rate since July 2024. The cooling in wage growth came from the services sector, where AHE increased 0.3% MoM and was up 3.6% YoY (down from 3.8% YoY). This was partially offset by a 0.4% MoM increase in goods sector AHE growth, which caused the annual rate to tick back up to 4.0% YoY. Overall, wage growth does continue to be a bit sticky, as it hasn’t returned to the post-pandemic lows of last summer, but since it is in a gradual decline, it isn’t a focus for the Fed, which is more concerned about layoffs picking up, leading to a higher unemployment rate.

Market Reaction

Markets reacted to the large disappointment in the headline job gains number as one might expect. The moves across asset classes reflected an increase in rate cut expectations, similar to moves seen earlier this week when weak numbers were also released in the JOLTS survey and the ADP employment report.

  • Equity futures reacted positively to the soft jobs report, rising in the hour or so after the release by about 0.4%. Futures trended around there until market open, when trading started to move lower. Two hours after the jobs report was released, the S&P 500 was down around -0.4%, and the NASDAQ gains were erased. Choppy trading can happen when increased rate cut expectations come up against a higher probability of a recession. Since the market has more or less judged a September rate cut as certain, that positive factor is likely being overwhelmed by recession fears in today’s trading.

  • The bond market and the US dollar are trading more one-dimensionally. The weakness in labor demand seen in this morning's report is leading bond traders to be more confident in a rate cut this month and in October and December. The large declines in the 1-year and 2-year rates, or around -11 to -12 bps, price in more rate cuts over the next six months. Recession fears are also hitting long-term yields as the 10-year yield drops -8 bps and the 30-year yield is down around -7 bps, reflecting lower growth expectations. The former is now moving towards 4.0% and is at its lowest since early April. The dollar is following yields, with the US dollar index (DXY) down -0.6% on the day, reflecting moves in EURUSD (+0.6%), GBPUSD (+0.6%), and USDJPY (-0.9%).

Today’s jobs report is the ultimate dovish finish to a week full of labor market updates. First, we had a larger-than-expected decline in job openings on Wednesday, next a disappointing print in the ADP employment report on Thursday, and now an even larger miss in the nonfarm payrolls number. Taken together, there is plenty of evidence to confirm the suspicions from the July jobs report revisions that the labor market cooling has continued, especially on the demand side in both the private and public sectors. Notably, the trend over the last few months is showing similar characteristics to the cooling that occurred in the summer of 2024, which resulted in a 50 bps rate cut.

Suddenly, there is a 73.4% chance that the Fed funds rate is at least 75 bps lower after the December FOMC meeting, according to CME Group’s FedWatch tool, which is up significantly from 45.8% just yesterday. The sharp turn in the probability doesn’t just reflect the softness in the labor market data this week, but it also incorporates the likelihood that the Trump administration successfully penetrates the Federal Reserve’s independence. In any case, investors should be prepared for the Fed to restart its cutting cycle in September and anticipate the policy rate dropping in the following 12 months.
